spec_match.rationale: Renders every value the spec accepts — `round`, `scoop`, `bevel`, `notch`, `straight`, `squircle`, and arbitrary `superellipse(N)` — with a live exponent slider. Complete coverage of the property's accepted values.

interactive.rationale: Range slider + select + button with 4 listeners drive a live preview as the user sweeps through values; CSS-driven for a CSS feature.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises `Every value the spec accepts ... with a live exponent slider that morphs the curve from circle to square`. Page is exactly that.

edge_cases.rationale: All named values + the superellipse continuum covered, but no demonstration of mixing values per corner (`corner-top-left-shape: scoop; corner-bottom-right-shape: notch`) which is where the API surface gets exotic.

references.rationale: `see also` now cites `https://chromestatus.com/feature/5357329815699456` and the CSS Borders 4 corner-shaping spec section.

design_system.rationale: 34 CSS variable references, no raw hex, no button override mismatch.

openQuestions.title: Add a feature-detect probe
openQuestions.detail: Unlike the sibling pages, this one has no probe. Add `CSS.supports('corner-shape', 'squircle')` with a banner so unsupported browsers know.

openQuestions.title: Add per-corner mixing examples
openQuestions.detail: The most surprising authoring shape is mixing values on different corners. Add a `mix corners` preset where each corner has a different value to show the longhand surface in one frame.

openQuestions.title: Surface the spec's range for `superellipse()`
openQuestions.detail: Document the allowed exponent range (0–∞ in theory; effectively 0.1–10 practically) on the slider and what each endpoint looks like, so authors know where the bounds are.

summary: Best-coverage page for the property's value surface — all named values plus the continuum — and the required ChromeStatus/CSS Borders references are now present. Remaining work is the feature-detect probe, per-corner mixing, and clearer superellipse bounds.
